symbols: outstretched hand of Christ — the divine command that conquers death, the word that calls the dead to life
bound figure of Lazarus — humanity enslaved by death, awaiting liberation by the Savior
kneeling woman — faith and supplication before Christ, the posture of intercession
recoiling onlookers — the awe and fear that accompany the manifestation of divine power over death
open tomb area — the threshold between death and life, foreshadowing Christ's own empty tomb
description: A large crowd surrounds Christ, who stands at center-left wearing a pale pink robe and blue mantle, His right hand raised in a commanding gesture toward Lazarus. Lazarus, partially wrapped in burial cloths, is supported by attendants at center-right, his body limp and pale. A woman in a golden-yellow dress kneels at Christ's feet. Other figures react with astonishment, some covering their noses. A sweeping landscape with classical ruins and green hills unfolds in the background under a clouded sky.
theological_description: Sebastiano del Piombo's monumental rendering of the raising of Lazarus presents the supreme sign (semeion) of Jesus' ministry in the Fourth Gospel — the seventh and greatest miracle that precipitates the plot to kill Him. John 11:25 provides the theological core: 'I am the resurrection and the life.' The scene captures the precise moment of Jesus' authoritative command, 'Lazarus, come forth!' (John 11:43). The kneeling woman — likely Mary of Bethany — embodies the faith that Christ demanded of Martha: 'Did I not tell you that if you believed you would see the glory of God?' (John 11:40). Patristic exegesis, especially Augustine and Cyril of Alexandria, understood this miracle as a proleptic sign of the general resurrection and as Christ's demonstration that He possessed the same life-giving power as the Father (John 5:21). The recoiling crowd, some covering their faces from the stench, anchors the narrative realism of Lazarus being four days dead — making the miracle unmistakable. Typologically, the unbinding of Lazarus prefigures the ultimate liberation from death accomplished at Easter.

Christ's declaration 'I am the resurrection and the life,' the theological heart of the scene
The Son gives life to whom He wills, as the Father raises the dead — demonstrated visually here
